title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: VR is the ultimate empathy machine. Imagine a history student 'standing' in a trench in WWI or a biology student 'shrinking' to enter a human cell. This level of immersion creates a lasting emotional and intellectual connection that textbooks can never replicate. Critics argue it is a distraction, but they said the same about the internet and the calculator. VR doesn't replace the teacher; it provides them with a superpower to make abstract concepts tangible and unforgettable.
texts.B: The cost of implementing VR at scale is prohibitive and will only worsen the 'digital divide' between rich and poor schools. Furthermore, I share the concern that over-reliance on 'gamified' learning reduces the student's ability to focus on difficult, non-visual tasks like reading and abstract logic. Education should be about training the mind to process information, not just providing a high-tech spectacle. We are raising a generation that can only learn if they are being 'entertained' by a headset.
texts.C: I agree that VR can be an incredible tool for 'visual' subjects, but I share the skepticism regarding its impact on social development. If students are wearing headsets for hours, they are missing out on the peer-to-peer eye contact and body language that are essential for emotional intelligence. However, I disagree that it is just 'entertainment.' When used correctly, VR can assist students with neurodivergent needs by providing a controlled, sensory-friendly environment that a traditional classroom lacks.
texts.D: The real potential of VR is not in 'spectacle,' but in 'collaboration.' It allows students from different parts of the world to work together in a shared virtual space, breaking down cultural barriers. While I share the concern about the cost, I believe prices will fall as the technology matures. I disagree with the idea that it hinders abstract thought; rather, it allows students to visualize the abstract. A student who can 'see' a four-dimensional shape or a complex chemical reaction is more likely to understand the theory behind it, not less.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether VR hinders the understanding of abstract concepts?
questions.explanation: Expert B thinks it reduces focus on 'abstract logic.' Expert D argues it actually helps students 'visualize the abstract' and understand the theory behind it.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the unique 'immersion' and emotional power of VR?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls VR an 'empathy machine' with 'lasting emotional connection.' Expert C also agrees it is an 'incredible tool' that provides a unique 'sensory-friendly environment.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the financial barriers to VR education?
questions.explanation: Expert B highlights the 'prohibitive cost' and the 'digital divide.' Expert D also 'shares the concern about the cost,' although they are more optimistic about future prices.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the social impact of using VR in schools?
questions.explanation: Expert C fears a loss of 'peer-to-peer eye contact' and social development. Expert D argues it actually fosters 'collaboration' and allows students to work together globally.
